Taking Shortcuts for Categorical VQA Using
Super Neurons
Pierre Musacchio1, Jaeyi Jeong2, Dahun Kim3, and Jaesik Park1
1Seoul National University
2EPFL
3Google Deepmind
{pmusacchio, jaesik.park}@snu.ac.kr
Abstract.Sparse Attention Vectors (SAVs) have emerged as an excel-
lent training-free alternative to supervised finetuning or low-rank adap-
tationtoimprovetheperformanceofvision-languagemodels(VLMs).At
their heart, SAVs select a few accurate attention heads for a task of inter-
est and use them as classifiers, rather than relying on the model’s predic-
tion. In a similar spirit, we find that directly probing the raw activations
of the VLM, in the form of scalar values, is sufficient to yield accurate
classifiers on diverse visually grounded downstream tasks. Shifting focus
from attention vectors to scalar activations dramatically increases the
search space for accurate parameters, allowing us to find more discrimi-
native neurons immediately from the first generated token. We call such
activations Super Neurons (SNs). In this probing setting, we discover
that enough SNs appear in the shallower layers of the large language
model to allow for extreme early exiting from the first layer of the model
at the first generated token. Compared to the original network, SNs ro-
bustly improve the classification performance while achieving a speedup
of up to 5.10 ×.
1 Introduction
Vision-languagemodel(VLM)arefrontiermodelsextendingthegenerativecapa-
bilities of large language model (LLM) via visual grounding [1,6,23,25]. Usually
consisting of billions of parameters, these models retain extensive knowledge
from internet-scale pretraining [4,8,30,33,39]. Although remarkably effective,
their complexity hinders attempts to understand how they operate at their core.
Current research on VLM explainability and efficiency improvement mainly
focuses on what could be calledmacro-level representations. That is, multi-
dimensional representations that are learned through aggregating information
from interactions of the tokens in the model. The most famous examples lie in
linear probing [35,42] or attention map extraction [17,27]. However, thanks to
the over-parameterization of current state of the art networks, we hypothesize
that models accumulate such a tremendous amount of information over training
that their individual activation scalars are sufficient to provide accurate answers
to specific questions. We term thesemicro-level representations. arXiv:2603.10781v1  [cs.CV]  11 Mar 2026

Our contributions are summarized as follows:
–We shift the analysis frommacro-level representations (akin to attention
vectors) tomicro-level ones (scalar activations). By doing so, we present a
training-free approach that identifies high-scoring neurons in the LLM of the
VLMs,
–We comprehensively benchmark the probed neurons and find that they can
serve as strong categorical classifiers,outperforming the base models them-
selveson a diverse suite of VQA benchmarks. We therefore call themSuper
Neurons,
–We thoroughly investigate SNs (discriminative power, location in the model,
quantity,robustness)andintroducetheagreement ratemetricthatquantifies
the divergence between SNs predictions and model predictions,
–As a byproduct, SNs enable extreme early exit at inference time, providing
a speedup of up to 5.10×while maintaining model-level performance.

Super Neurons 3
2 Related work
Efficient VLMs.A conventional approach to turn large VLMs to efficient
models is to prune them at the parameter level, either by distillation [40] or
by training a policy to search which weights to remove [22]. Pruning can also
occur at the token level, usually via token similarity approaches [5,14,41,43],
by estimating visual contribution [24], or using scale-down approaches [25]. If
the final objective is to improve performance, training a robust visual encoder is
also a viable solution [9,37]. Some approaches diverge by considering early exit
from the model, either in a supervised setting [2] or in a training-free manner
by estimating layer-wise similarities [36]. Recently, task vectors [11] have been
leveraged in VLMs in the form of sparse attention vectors to enable training-free
improvement of VLMs in classification tasks [27]. Single modality convnets and
LSTMs can rely on some of their weights for accurate prediction [19,32], but this
remains to be shown for transformers, specifically when processing multimodal
tokens.

Although we solely focus
on categorical VQA tasks, we
propose a training-free ap-

first layer.
Explainable VLMs.Model explainability is a fundamental challenge for the
deployment of VLMs in the real world. Since VLMs are increasingly being

adopted as master operators in robotics [3,18,44], guardrails must be set up
to ensure the security of their behavior. Substantial work has led to a better
understanding of how attention, which VLMs are usually built on, behaves. No-
tably, CLIP-Dissects proposes tagging each neuron in the transformer with a
concept [29], showing that transformers learn more complex patterns as the rep-
resentation is forwarded down its layers. Efforts have also been directed towards
understanding attention sinks [7,16,31]. Moreover, due to the extensive number
of attention operations in the LLM of the VLM, the community has reported
the emergence of object-aligned attention maps in the transformer decoder of
the architecture [17]. Linear probing approaches tend to show that the VLM
generates its answer based on different stages of reasoning [42], yet, these stages
do not seem to be monolithic [27,35]. Sparse autoencoders has shown that some
specific neurons hold object-specific concepts [13,38].
In our work, we propose studying the capabilities of individual neurons with-
out adding a single learning component that could alter the understanding of
their function. By repurposing raw activations as categorical predictions, we
show that VLMs possess expert neurons across a diverse set of tasks. Analyzing
the location of the emergence of these neurons helps us better understand that
the LLM is in principal capable of answering a question sometimes as early as
in thefirst layerof the LLM when generating thefirst tokenof the answer.
We also investigate to what extent SNs and the model disagree by introducing
the agreement rate (AR) metric. Robustness experiments suggest that SNs are
notexploiting spurious correlations of the input data and generalize to new dis-
tributions or neighboring prompts, suggesting the universality of our approach.

3 Method
3.1 Preliminaries
Notations.We define a VLM as the combination of vision and text encoders
Evis:RH×W×3→Rsvis×DandEtxt:Ns→Rstxt×Dthat feed their output
to an LLM f:R(svis+stxt+1)×D→R(svis+stxt+1)×D. Given a grounding image
I∈RH×W×3and a text prompt T∈Ns, a VLM forward pass is defined as
follows:
ˆYs+1=f(Evis(I);Etxt(T)). (1)
Here, ˆYs+1can be auto-regressively fed into f. This process ends when the LLM
generates an<eos>token. Moreover, given an L∈Nlayered LLM, we denote
Hl∈RDthe activation extracted from the l-th layer. For clarity, we omit the
subscript when referring to the full set of Lactivations,i.e. H∈RL×D.
Problem.ConventionalVLMsarebuiltfromanLLMarchitecturethatcontains
billions of parameters, processing both vision and text tokens. We hypothesize
that this parametric scale is reasonable for individual neurons to hold critical
information about the answer for a given text-image pair and that we do not
necessarily need the full model to answer the question. Specifically, we claim
thatsome scalar activations are sufficient to provide a satisfactory answer, on
par or even better than the full model itself. We call these hypothetical neuron
outputsSuper Neurons(SNs). Inspired by [27], our work provides a simple setup
to discover such SNs for categorical VQA.
Uncovering SNs can be thought of as a three-step process. First, we gather
aprobing set. We then perform a forward pass of the network on the probing
set to uncover neurons that have high activations on it based on a metric to
optimize. Finally, we evaluate SNs on the validation set of the dataset to assess
their performance. We provide the complete algorithm used to discover SNs in
Algorithm 1.

5 Conclusion
We highlight that scalar activations deemed Super Neurons can serve as strong
and robust categorical classifiers while improving runtime efficiency of VLMs.
SNs compare favorably with the base models they are extracted from and sparse
attention vectors, while enabling discovery of a large amount of accurate scalars
in shallower layers of the LLM. This enablesextreme early exitingduring the
generation of thefirst tokenand in thefirst layerof the LLM, substantially
reducing inference time. We plan to apply our method for vision language action
models, in which accurate discrete action decisions could be taken faster.
Limitations.Future research should be conducted to determine whether Super
Neurons are able to provide useful signals on complex open-ended prompts and
reasoning and if robustness and early exiting still hold in these contexts.

A Datasets and prompts
We describe the dataset along with the prompts used for probing and validation
in more detail in this section.
Pope.Popeis a dataset designed to evaluate the degree of hallucinations of
VLMs. Built on top ofCoco, it consists of 2.9K VQA that relate to the presence
or absence of objects in images. Broadly speaking, to visual hallucinations [21].
InstaOrder (Occ.).InstaOrderalso stems fromCocoand consists of
instance-wise ordering annotations. Recent work converted this dataset into
a B-VQA format and observed that LLaVA-v1.5-7b struggles to answer such
geometry-related prompts. This split of the dataset tests the models’ under-
standing of instance-wise occlusions [20,28].
InstaOrder (Depth).Second split ofInstaOrder, the questions con-
tained in this challenge test the models’ abilities to reason about instance-wise
depth order.
VizWiz.Created by people with visual impairments,VizWizis a general
benchmark aiming at testing models’ capacities to reason under constrained
visual inputs. We filter the dataset on “yes”-“no” answers and only keep such
questions for both the probing and validation sets [10].
Clevr.To test our method’s generalization capabilities on synthetic data,
we selectClevr. This dataset evaluates the spatial understanding of the model
through questions that span from counting to positional understanding. We filter
this dataset to only retain classification questions for probing and validation [15].
A-OKVQA.A-OKVQAisanMCQdatasetofwithfouranswerchoicesconsist-
ing of general visual world knowledge. Questions require commonsense reasoning
grounded in the scene in order to be accurately answered [34].

Baseline configurations.We report the configurations of LLaVA-v1.5-7b and

not alter these configurations unless specified otherwise in the paper.
Finetuned baselines comparison.We compare Super Neurons to fully fine-
tuned and LoRA-tuned [12] LLaVA [23] at different dataset scales in Tab. 3.
We use a dataset on which LLaVA is known to struggle to clearly identify the
data scale at which finetuning begins to outperform SNs. For a small annotated
finetuning budget, SNs obtain better results than finetuned alternatives while
being training-free. On the other hand, as the annotation budget grows, fully
finetuning the model becomes a better alternative if the goal is to improve per-
formance for asingle task. In fact, finetuning the model on a single task can lead
to significant degradation on other tasks. In contrast, our training-free approach
maintains the model’s general capabilities by preserving the pretrained weights.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a novel approach to improving the performance of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by identifying "Super Neurons" (SNs) that can accurately classify categorical visual question answering (VQA) tasks. The approach involves probing the raw activations of the VLM and converting them into classification predictions, rather than relying on attention vectors or macro-level representations. The authors demonstrate that SNs can outperform the base model on a diverse suite of VQA benchmarks and enable extreme early exiting, resulting in a speedup of up to 5.10× while maintaining model-level performance. The document also reviews related work on efficient VLMs and model explainability, highlighting the potential of the proposed approach for real-world applications. Key contributions include the identification of SNs, their evaluation on various VQA tasks, and the introduction of a metric to quantify the divergence between SNs predictions and model predictions.

structure.title: Related work
structure.summary:
The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) in the context of Visual Language Models (VLMs). The main points covered include: 
1. Efficient VLMs: The document reviews existing approaches to make VLMs more efficient, such as pruning, distillation, and training robust visual encoders.
2. Introduction to Super Neurons: The authors propose a training-free approach to identify expert neural activations, called Super Neurons, which can solve tasks accurately and robustly without relying on token similarity or altering model weights.
3. Architecture comparison: The document compares the architecture of SNs with Sparse Attention Vectors (SAVs), highlighting the differences in their representation and search spaces.
4. Explainable VLMs: The authors discuss the importance of model explainability in VLMs, reviewing existing work on understanding attention and neural activations in transformers.
5. Methodology: The document outlines the methodology for discovering SNs, including a three-step process of gathering a probing set, performing a forward pass, and evaluating SNs on a validation set.
6. Algorithm: The authors provide two algorithms, one for extracting SNs and another for SNs inference, which enable the identification and utilization of SNs for categorical Visual Question Answering (VQA) tasks.

nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) in the context of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). The main points covered include the introduction of the Super Neurons algorithm, which aims to identify a subset of neurons in a VLM that can provide satisfactory answers to a given text-image pair. The document outlines a three-step process to discover SNs, including gathering a probing set, performing a forward pass to uncover neurons with high activations, and evaluating SNs on a validation set. The algorithm uses a threshold variable to binarize raw activations and evaluates each neuron using a predetermined metric to acquire neuron-level statistics. The document also introduces key notations, such as the vision and text encoders, the LLM, and the activation threshold parameter, and provides a mathematical formulation of the Super Neurons discovery process.

nodes.title: Results
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) in the context of Visual Language Models (VLMs). SNs refer to scalar activations in VLMs that can serve as strong and robust categorical classifiers. The document presents experimental results demonstrating the effectiveness of SNs in improving the runtime efficiency of VLMs while maintaining or improving their performance. The results show that SNs can outperform or match the performance of the base models they are extracted from, and they can also compare favorably with sparse attention vectors. The document also explores the scalability and universality of the SNs approach, demonstrating its applicability to larger models and different datasets. Additionally, the document discusses the robustness of SNs to transfer experiments and prompt changes, and it investigates the impact of token selection, data regime, and metric optimization on SNs performance. Overall, the document highlights the potential of SNs to enable extreme early exiting during the generation of the first token and in the first layer of the VLM, substantially reducing inference time.

nodes.title: Table 1: Prompt templates
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the use of prompt templates for various Visual Question Answering (VQA) datasets, including Pope, InstaOrder, A-OKVQA, and ScienceQa. It provides details on the templates used for each dataset, including variables for object categories, locations, and question answers. The document also outlines the default configurations for two models, LLaVA-v1.5-7b and Qwen3-VL-4b-Instruct, used for experiments. Additionally, it describes the ScienceQa dataset, a high school science dataset with 26 topics, 127 categories, and 379 skills, and how it is converted to binary visual question answers for inference. The use of prompts for different datasets, including Clevr and VizWiz, is also mentioned, with specific templates and filtering applied to retain only "yes"-"no" VQA samples.

nodes.title: Table 2: Default configurations
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the use of prompt templates for Visual Question Answering (VQA) datasets and the configuration of two models, LLaVA-v1.5-7b and Qwen3-VL-4b-Instruct. It also presents the results of experiments on the ScienceQa dataset and compares the performance of Super Neurons to finetuned LLaVA alternatives on the InstaOrder dataset. The document highlights the advantages of using Super Neurons, including training-free performance and maintained general capabilities. Additionally, it presents profiling benchmark results, demonstrating the efficiency of Super Neurons in reducing wall time to obtain an answer. The main points covered include VQA datasets, model configurations, experimental results, and the benefits of using Super Neurons.

nodes.title: B Baselines
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of Super Neurons (SNs) with finetuned LLaVA alternatives on the InstaOrder dataset. The main points covered include the evaluation of training time and task performance with varying annotation budgets, the comparison of SNs with fully finetuned and LoRA-tuned LLaVA models, and the profiling of SNs using an in-depth benchmark on an NVIDIA A100 GPU. The results show that SNs outperform finetuned alternatives with small annotation budgets while being training-free, and maintain the model's general capabilities by preserving pretrained weights. Additionally, SNs demonstrate significant speedup over the base model by bypassing the autoregressive process and post-processing routine.

nodes.title: Finetuned baselines comparison
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of Super Neurons (SNs) with finetuned LLaVA alternatives on the InstaOrder dataset. The main points covered include the evaluation of training time and task performance of SNs against finetuned LLaVA models with varying annotation budgets. The results show that SNs outperform finetuned alternatives with small annotation budgets while being training-free, but finetuning becomes a better option for larger budgets. The document also highlights the profiling benchmark of SNs, demonstrating their ability to dramatically decrease wall time to obtain an answer by bypassing the autoregressive process of the transformer and avoiding post-processing routines, making them 1.8 times faster than the base model.

nodes.title: C Profiling
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) and their comparison to finetuned LLaVA alternatives on the InstaOrder dataset. The main points covered include the evaluation of SNs against baseline models, the analysis of training time and task performance, and the robustness of SNs to dataset transfer and prompt sensitivity. The document also presents profiling benchmarks, demonstrating the efficiency of SNs in reducing wall time to obtain answers. Additionally, it investigates the sensitivity of SNs to prompts and their ability to encode general in-domain knowledge for a given task, as well as their potential for overfitting or exploiting spurious bias from input data. The results show that SNs outperform finetuned alternatives in certain scenarios, maintain the model's general capabilities, and are robust to prompt changes, but may fail to answer accurately when the prompt is not grounded in the image.

nodes.title: D Robustness
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the robustness and sensitivity of a language model, specifically LLaVA-v1.5-7b, to different prompts and datasets. The main points covered include the model's performance on various metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and recall, as well as its robustness to changes in prompt wording and dataset transfer. The document also investigates the model's sensitivity to prompts and its ability to encode general in-domain knowledge for a given task. Additionally, it explores the potential for overfitting or exploiting spurious bias from input data through adversarial prompting experiments on the Pope dataset.

nodes.title: Prompt robustness
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) and their application in various tasks. The main points covered include the robustness of SNs to prompt changes, their ability to generalize to novel distributions, and their performance on different datasets. The document also investigates the sensitivity of SNs to prompts and their potential for overfitting or exploiting spurious biases. Additionally, it visualizes the location of SNs on different datasets and discusses their usefulness in improving model performance while reducing runtime. The results are presented in various tables and figures, showcasing the effectiveness of SNs in tasks such as comparative reasoning and visual question answering.

nodes.title: Adversarial prompting
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the evaluation of Super Neurons (SNs) on adversarial prompting datasets, specifically Pope-Im and Pope-Txt validation datasets. It presents results on the performance of SNs using mean and majority voting methods, highlighting their accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores. The document also visualizes the location of SNs on different datasets, showing that few SNs emerge on well-answered datasets, while many appear on harder datasets. Additionally, it emphasizes that SNs can generalize to novel distributions and are not leveraging spurious biases or overfitting to come up with their decisions, making them useful for improving model performance while reducing runtime.

The document also visualizes the location of SNs on different datasets, including Pope and Vizwiz, and discusses how SNs can generalize to novel distributions and improve model performance while reducing runtime. nodes.title: Dataset-wise
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) and their evaluation on various datasets, including Pope-Im and Pope-Txt. The results show that SNs can achieve high accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores, and can generalize to novel distributions. The document also visualizes the location of SNs on different datasets, showing that few SNs emerge on well-answered datasets, while many SNs appear on harder datasets. Additionally, the document explores the overlap of SNs across different datasets, finding that some neurons capture useful information across multiple tasks, and hypothesizes potential links between different types of questions. Overall, the document highlights the potential of Super Neurons as a lightweight and powerful representation that can generalize across diverse types of data.

nodes.title: Cross-dataset
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Super Neurons (SNs) in the context of neural networks, specifically the LLaVA-v1.5-7b model. The main points covered include the observation that datasets where the model struggles tend to have more SNs, often located in shallower layers. The document also explores the overlap of SNs across different datasets, finding that some neurons capture useful information across multiple tasks and suggesting potential links between different types of questions. The authors identify specific datasets, such as InstaOrder and Clevr, that share a large number of SNs, and highlight cases where single neurons surpass the model's performance on multiple benchmarks. The document concludes by emphasizing the potential of sparse activations in the form of Super Neurons as a lightweight and powerful representation that can generalize across diverse types of data.
